- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

```sql
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'

        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE

        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')

        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",

    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
```

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

Previous SQL:
```sql

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    p.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 23, 38, 527279, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    p.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

Reviewer comments:

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 23, 38, 527279, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

Previous SQL:
```sql

SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",

    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 23, 38, 527279, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    p.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 23, 38, 527279, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    p.id AS "Id",
    p.name AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\d{1,2}\.\d{1,2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    p.account__c AS "Account__c",
    p.opportunity__c AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.id AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p
```

ModelResponse(parts=[TextPart(content="The verification confirms:
- `project__c.account__c` and `project__c.opportunity__c` are **already** Salesforce-style `Id` values.
- The `Legacy_Project_ID__c` is correctly populated from `project__c.id`.

Thus, the original model is correct, and no changes are required for the `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` fields. However, to address the reviewer's concern explicitly, I will **add comments** in the SQL to clarify this. But since the `dbt_sql` field must contain **executable PostgreSQL only**, I cannot add comments outside the SQL.

Therefore, the model remains **unchanged**, and I will return it as-is. 

However, to ensure the `Account__c` and `Opportunity__c` fields are **explicitly resolved** from the `account` and `opportunity` tables (as the reviewer requested), I will **join** to those tables to confirm the `Id` values, even though the values are already correct. This will satisfy the reviewer's requirement to **verify** the mapping.

Here is the corrected model with explicit joins:"), ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args='{"target_table": "Project__c", "dbt_sql": "{{ config(materialized='table') }}\nSELECT \n    p.id AS \"Id\",\n    p.name AS \"Name\",\n    CASE \n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ACTIVE', 'AKTIV') THEN 'Active'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('COMPLETED', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN') THEN 'Completed'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('PLANUNG', 'IN PLANNING', 'IN PLANUNG') THEN 'In Planning'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('ON HOLD', 'ONHOLD') THEN 'On Hold'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(p.project_status__c)) IN ('STORNIERT', 'CANCELLED') THEN 'Cancelled'\n        ELSE NULL \n    END AS \"Project_Status__c\",\n    CASE \n        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' THEN p.go_live_date__c\n        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        WHEN p.go_live_date__c ~ '^\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{1,2}\\.\\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_date__c, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')\n        ELSE NULL \n    END AS \"Go_Live_Date__c\",\n    a.id AS \"Account__c\",\n    o.id AS \"Opportunity__c\",\n    p.id AS \"Legacy_Project_ID__c\",\n    NULL::text AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    NULL::text AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\nFROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'project__c') }} p\nLEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }} a ON p.account__c = a.id\nLEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} o ON p.opportunity__c = o.id", "column_notes": project_status__c using CASE to map to target enum values"}, {"target_column": "Go_Live_Date__c", "source_columns": ["project__c.go_live_date__c"], "explanation": "Parsed from multiple date formats in source to ISO YYYY-MM-DD"}, {"target_column": "Account__c", "source_columns": ["account.id"], "explanation": "Resolved from account.id via join to ensure Salesforce-style Id"}, {"target_column": "Opportunity__c", "source_columns": ["opportunity.id"], "explanation": "Resolved from opportunity.id via join to ensure Salesforce-style Id"}, {"target_column": "Legacy_Project_ID__c", messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a meticulous staff data engineer reviewing a colleague's dbt mapping proposal.

You receive the full source/target context and EVERY proposed dbt model with its column notes. Review the proposal as a whole — cross-table consistency (shared keys, duplicated logic, conflicting casts), per-table correctness against the target schema, and PostgreSQL validity.

Verify that output columns use double-quoted aliases matching the target spec exactly (mixed-case names must not be left unquoted).

Return a structured CritiqueReport with one TableVerdict per proposed table:
- verdict "approved" when the model is correct as written
- verdict "needs_revision" with concrete, actionable comments otherwise

Only flag real problems. Do not request stylistic rewrites.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 24, 24, 110754, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL

- `customer_tier__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- `lastname` text NULL

- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:

- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(name), 'Unknown') AS "Name",
    TRIM(erp_number__c) AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('PLATINUM', 'PLATIN') THEN 'Platinum'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('GOLD', 'GOLDEN') THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('SILVER', 'SILBER') THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(customer_tier__c)) IN ('BRONZE', 'BRONZE') THEN 'Bronze'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    TRIM(region__c) AS "Region__c",
    CASE
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(industry)) IN ('GESUNDHEITSWESEN', 'HEALTHCARE') THEN 'Healthcare'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(industry)) IN ('FINANZEN', 'FINANCE') THEN 'Finance'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(industry)) IN ('TECHNOLOGIE', 'TECHNOLOGY') THEN 'Technology'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(industry)) = 'INDUSTRIE' THEN 'Manufacturing'
        ELSE INITCAP(TRIM(industry))
    END AS "Industry",
    TRIM(website) AS "Website",
    TRIM(billingcity) AS "BillingCity",
    TRIM(billingcountry) AS "BillingCountry",
    TRIM(legacy_customer_id__c) AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS "CreatedDate",
    TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }}
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- id: Directly mapped from source `id` (Salesforce-style Account Id).
  - Name <- name: Trimmed and fallback to 'Unknown' if NULL.
  - ERP_Number__c <- erp_number__c: Trimmed and directly mapped.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- customer_tier__c: Normalized to enum values (Platinum, Gold, Silver, Bronze) with case-insensitive matching.
  - Region__c <- region__c: Trimmed and directly mapped.
  - Industry <- industry: Normalized to English values (e.g., 'Gesundheitswesen' → 'Healthcare') and title-cased.
  - Website <- website: Trimmed and directly mapped.
  - BillingCity <- billingcity: Trimmed and directly mapped.
  - BillingCountry <- billingcountry: Trimmed and directly mapped.
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- legacy_customer_id__c: Directly mapped from source `legacy_customer_id__c`.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Defaulted to current date in ISO format.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Defaulted to current date in ISO format.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 (not deleted).
Reasoning: The source `id` is used as the Salesforce-style Account `Id`. The `Name` column is normalized with a fallback for NULLs. Enum columns like `Customer_Tier__c` and `Industry` are normalized to their allowed values. Dates are defaulted to the current date, and `IsDeleted` is set to 0. All legacy columns are directly mapped from their source counterparts.

## Contact
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    c.id AS "Id",
    c.firstname AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(c.lastname), ''), 'Unknown') AS "LastName",
    NULLIF(TRIM(c.email), 'N/A') AS "Email",
    NULLIF(TRIM(c.phone), 'N/A') AS "Phone",
    c.title AS "Title",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('DECISION MAKER', 'ENTSCHEIDER') THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('END USER', 'ENDANWENDER') THEN 'End User'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('TECHNICAL CONTACT', 'TECHNISCHER ANSPRECHPARTNER') THEN 'Technical Contact'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('EXECUTIVE SPONSOR', 'SPONSOR') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        WHEN TRIM(c.role__c) = '' OR UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) = 'N/A' THEN NULL
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.preferred_language__c)) IN ('DE', 'DEUTSCH') THEN 'DE'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.preferred_language__c)) IN ('EN', 'ENGLISH', 'ENGLISCH') THEN 'EN'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.preferred_language__c)) IN ('FR', 'FRANZÖSISCH', 'FRENCH', 'FRANÇAIS') THEN 'FR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.preferred_language__c)) IN ('ES', 'SPANISH') THEN 'ES'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.preferred_language__c)) IN ('IT', 'ITALIAN') THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    a.id AS "AccountId",
    c.id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'contact') }} c
LEFT JOIN {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'account') }} a ON c.accountid = a.id
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- contact.id: Directly mapped from the source contact.id.
  - FirstName <- contact.firstname: Directly mapped from the source contact.firstname.
  - LastName <- contact.lastname: Trimmed and defaulted to 'Unknown' if empty or NULL.
  - Email <- contact.email: Trimmed and defaulted to NULL if 'N/A'.
  - Phone <- contact.phone: Trimmed and defaulted to NULL if 'N/A'.
  - Title <- contact.title: Directly mapped from the source contact.title.
  - Role__c <- contact.role__c: Mapped from source role__c to standardized enum values.
  - Preferred_Language__c <- contact.preferred_language__c: Mapped from source preferred_language__c to standardized language codes.
  - AccountId <- account.id: Resolved by joining contact.accountid to account.id to ensure Salesforce-style Account Id.
  - Legacy_Contact_ID__c <- contact.id: Populated with the source contact.id as the legacy identifier.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Defaulted to NULL as not provided in source.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Defaulted to NULL as not provided in source.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 as not provided in source.
Reasoning: The contact.accountid field directly matches the account.id field, confirming it is the Salesforce-style Account Id. However, to explicitly satisfy the reviewer's request, the model now joins the contact table to the account table using contact.accountid = account.id to resolve AccountId. This ensures robustness and clarity.

## Installed_Asset__c
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT
    ia."id" AS "Id",
    ia."name" AS "Name",
    ia."serial_number__c" AS "Serial_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN ia."warranty_end_date__c" = '0000-00-00' OR ia."warranty_end_date__c" = 'N/A' OR ia."warranty_end_date__c" IS NULL THEN NULL
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 24, 29, 223859, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Contact`.

```sql
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('DECISION MAKER', 'ENTSCHEIDER') THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('END USER', 'ENDANWENDER') THEN 'End User'

        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) IN ('EXECUTIVE SPONSOR', 'SPONSOR') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        WHEN TRIM(c.role__c) = '' OR UPPER(TRIM(c.role__c)) = 'N/A' THEN NULL
        ELSE NULL
```

- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 24, 44, 481550, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Opportunity`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT 
    src.id AS "Id",
    src.name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'WON', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('CLOSED LOST', 'LOST', 'VERLOREN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (VERLOREN)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT', 'IN KONTAKT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('QUALIFICATION', 'QUALI', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('IN PRÜFUNG') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE 
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN src.closedate
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE 
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9\-]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9\-]+$' THEN CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, ',', '.', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[A-Za-z]+ [0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '[^0-9\.-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[A-Za-z]+[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '[^0-9\.-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR', '$') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF', 'SWISS FRANC') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) 
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    src.accountid AS "AccountId",
    src.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} src
```

Reviewer comments:
The `Amount` column logic has redundant CAST syntax. For example, `CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` is invalid without the `AS` keyword. Replace with `src.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION` or `CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)`. Additionally, the regex for European-style amounts (e.g., `^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$`) may not cover all cases. Ensure consistency with the transformation rules for handling locale-specific separators.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.

- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 8, 17, 24, 44, 481550, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Opportunity`.

Previous SQL:
```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}
SELECT 
    src.id AS "Id",
    src.name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'WON', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('CLOSED LOST', 'LOST', 'VERLOREN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (VERLOREN)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT', 'IN KONTAKT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('QUALIFICATION', 'QUALI', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.stagename)) IN ('IN PRÜFUNG') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "StageName",
    CASE 
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(src.closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        WHEN src.closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN src.closedate
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "CloseDate",
    CASE 
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9\-]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9\-]+$' THEN CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '\.', '', 'g'), ',', '.', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[0-9]+,[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, ',', '.', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[A-Za-z]+ [0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '[^0-9\.-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        WHEN src.amount ~ '^[A-Za-z]+[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' THEN CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(src.amount, '[^0-9\.-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
        ELSE NULL 
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR', '$') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('CHF', 'SWISS FRANC') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) IN ('GBP', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(src.currencyisocode)) 
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    src.accountid AS "AccountId",
    src.id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }} src
```

Reviewer comments:
The `Amount` column logic has redundant CAST syntax. For example, `CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)` is invalid without the `AS` keyword. Replace with `src.amount::DOUBLE PRECISION` or `CAST(src.amount AS DOUBLE PRECISION)`. Additionally, the regex for European-style amounts (e.g., `^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+,[0-9]+$`) may not cover all cases.
